claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 19 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 19 and in approved education
  - Another claimant who lives with the child has priority
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child in hospital for 11 weeks (within 12-week limit)
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child works 24+ hours/week but is in approved education
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Another claimant who lives with the child has priority
circumstances: Child is 19 and in approved education
Another claimant who lives with the child has priority
Child is not in local authority care
Child in hospital for 11 weeks (within 12-week limit)
Child works 24+ hours/week but is in approved education
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. To assess your eligibility, I'll need to ask you some questions about your circumstances.

Let me start with: **Do you have any children you're responsible for, and if so, how many children are you asking about?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I have one child I'm responsible for. His name is Alex, and I'd like to know if I'm eligible to claim Child Benefit for him.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! I'd like to help you determine if you're eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex.

Let me start with some basic questions:

**How old is Alex?**

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you. Since Alex is 19 years old, his eligibility depends on whether he's in approved education or training, as Child Benefit can continue until age 20 in certain circumstances.

**Is Alex currently in approved education or training?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, he is. He's studying for his A-levels at the moment.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
That's helpful to know. Since Alex is 19 and studying for his A-levels, which is approved education, that's positive for eligibility.

Now I need to ask about your living situation:

**Does Alex live with you, or if he doesn't live with you, are you contributing at least the same amount as Child Benefit would be (or the equivalent in kind) towards his upkeep - such as food, clothes, or pocket money?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, Alex lives with me at home.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Excellent. Now I need to confirm a few more things:

**Do you live in the UK?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Just a couple more questions to complete the assessment:

**Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, actually someone else in the household is already claiming Child Benefit for him.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that important information. 

**Who is currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex, and are you looking to take over the claim from them, or are you asking if you would be eligible if they stopped claiming?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: It's someone else in the household who lives with Alex. I'm just checking if I would be eligible to claim for him.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying. Let me also check one final detail:

**Has Alex started any paid work for 24 hours or more per week, or is he claiming any benefits in his own right (such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance)?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: He does work quite a bit - he has a full-time job alongside his studies, so yes, he works 24 hours or more per week. But he's not claiming any benefits in his own name.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing all that information. Based on what you've told me, I'm afraid **you would not be eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex**.

Here's why:

While Alex meets some of the criteria - he's under 20, studying for his A-levels (which is approved education), and lives with you in the UK - unfortunately, he's **working 24 hours or more per week in paid employment**. 

According to the Child Benefit rules, you'll stop getting Child Benefit if your child starts paid work for 24 hours or more a week while they're in approved education or training. This rule applies even though Alex is still studying.

So even if the other person in your household were to stop claiming, you would not be able to claim Child Benefit for Alex while he continues to work 24 or more hours per week.

If Alex's work situation changes in the future (for example, if he reduces his hours to below 24 per week), the eligibility situation could be different.

reasonings: Alex is 19 years old and studying for his A-levels, which would normally be considered approved education that allows Child Benefit to continue until age 20. Alex lives with the claimant in the UK, which satisfies the residency and responsibility requirements. However, according to the Child Benefit rules, you'll stop getting Child Benefit if your child starts paid work for 24 hours or more a week and is no longer in approved education or training. Since Alex works 24 hours or more per week in a full-time job alongside his studies, he does not qualify for Child Benefit regardless of his educational status. Therefore, the claimant would not be eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant meets the basic eligibility criteria (lives in the UK, Alex lives with them, and Alex is under 20 and in education). However, Alex is working 24 hours or more per week in paid employment, which disqualifies him from Child Benefit eligibility even though he is still studying. Therefore, the claimant is not eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex.

child_evaluations.reasoning: Alex is 19 years old and studying for his A-levels, which would normally be considered approved education that allows Child Benefit to continue until age 20. Alex lives with the claimant in the UK, which satisfies the residency and responsibility requirements. However, according to the Child Benefit rules, you'll stop getting Child Benefit if your child starts paid work for 24 hours or more a week and is no longer in approved education or training. Since Alex works 24 hours or more per week in a full-time job alongside his studies, he does not qualify for Child Benefit regardless of his educational status. Therefore, the claimant would not be eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant meets the basic eligibility criteria (lives in the UK, Alex lives with them, and Alex is under 20 and in education). However, Alex is working 24 hours or more per week in paid employment, which disqualifies him from Child Benefit eligibility even though he is still studying. Therefore, the claimant is not eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex.
